proposed_hypotheses.text: Among the binary features in this AML cohort, at least one binary feature is associated with a higher objective_response rate compared to patients without that feature.

analyses.code: for c in binary_cols: chi2_contingency(pd.crosstab(df[c], df['objective_response']))
analyses.result_summary: Chi-square screen of all 80 binary features vs objective_response. The strongest association is feature_035: response rate 22.13% in feature_035=1 (n=3624) vs 16.50% in feature_035=0 (n=46376), absolute difference +5.63 percentage points, chi-square p=3.5e-18. Other notable hits: feature_093 (+1.7 pts, p=5.6e-3), feature_121 (-1.4 pts, p=2.5e-2), feature_014 (+0.7 pts, p=3.2e-2).

proposed_hypotheses.text: At least one continuous feature is associated with objective_response, with a monotonic direction across its distribution.

analyses.code: for c in cont_cols: pointbiserialr(df['objective_response'], df[c])
analyses.result_summary: Point-biserial correlation screen. feature_011 strongly negatively associated (mean 3.38 in responders vs 3.95 in non-responders, r=-0.055, p=4.6e-35). feature_006 also negative (mean 58.23 vs 60.31, r=-0.041, p=1.0e-19). Smaller effects: feature_099 positive (r=+0.022, p=1.4e-6), feature_063 negative (r=-0.021, p=2.9e-6), feature_092 negative (r=-0.019, p=1.9e-5). Quintile analysis confirmed monotonic gradients for feature_011 (response rate 18.6% in lowest quintile to 13.3% in highest) and feature_006 (18.7% in lowest to 14.9% in highest).

proposed_hypotheses.text: objective_response rate differs by patient race (feature_005), with white patients having higher response rates than non-white patients.
objective_response rate differs by insurance type (feature_087).

analyses.result_summary: Race-specific response rates: white 17.28% (n=32498), hispanic 16.70% (n=7631), asian 16.33% (n=2970), black 15.74% (n=5889), other 14.72% (n=1012). Chi-square p=9.6e-3. White patients have a small but significant elevation; signal is consistent with the proposed direction.
Insurance-specific response rates: medicare 17.18%, medicaid 16.87%, private 16.66%, uninsured 16.62%. Differences are small; chi-square p=0.54. No meaningful difference by insurance type.

proposed_hypotheses.text: Patient age (feature_078, range 18-92, mean ~68) is associated with objective_response, with older patients having lower response rates (a known prognostic pattern in AML).

analyses.result_summary: feature_078 mean is 67.9 (sd 10.9, range 18-92), consistent with patient age in years. However, point-biserial correlation with objective_response is essentially zero (r=-0.001, p=0.83). Quintile response rates are flat across age strata (16.7%, 17.0%, 17.1%, 16.8%, 17.0%). Hypothesis is REFUTED: feature_078 has no association with response in this cohort.

proposed_hypotheses.text: In a multivariable logistic regression simultaneously adjusting for the strongest univariate predictors (feature_035, feature_011, feature_006, feature_092, feature_063, feature_099, feature_078, race, insurance), feature_035 retains a positive association with objective_response.
After multivariable adjustment, the univariate race association attenuates, suggesting the race effect is confounded by clinical features.

analyses.result_summary: Multivariable logit. feature_035 coefficient +0.366 (OR=1.44, p=3.8e-18); feature_011 -0.041 (p<1e-30); feature_006 -0.006 (p<1e-19); feature_063 -0.007 (p<1e-5); feature_092 -0.002 (p<1e-4); feature_099 +0.117 (p<1e-5); feature_093 +0.120 (p=5e-3). feature_078 (age) p=0.77 (no effect). All race indicator p-values >0.15 after adjustment (white p=0.16); insurance p>0.5. h6 SUPPORTED. h7 SUPPORTED: race no longer significant once clinical features are included.

proposed_hypotheses.text: After adjusting for the top five continuous predictors, additional binary features emerge with significant effects on objective_response that were not detectable in the univariate screen.

analyses.result_summary: Adjusted-by-continuous binary screen. Significant adjusted hits (alpha 0.05): feature_035 (coef +0.366, OR 1.44, p=3.8e-18); feature_093 (+0.118, OR 1.13, p=5.8e-3); feature_121 (-0.101, OR 0.90, p=2.4e-2); feature_014 (+0.054, OR 1.06, p=2.6e-2); feature_111 (-0.081, OR 0.92, p=4.7e-2); feature_012 (-0.048, OR 0.95, p=4.8e-2). h8 partially supported: same features as univariate, no new ones emerged after adjustment.

proposed_hypotheses.text: The effect of feature_035 on objective_response varies by race (feature_005), suggesting biological or treatment-access heterogeneity.

analyses.code: logit with feature_035 * race interaction terms
analyses.result_summary: Within-race feature_035 effects: white +6.46 pts (p=2.3e-15), hispanic +4.10 pts (p=1.4e-2), black +4.09 pts (p=3.0e-2), asian +3.96 pts (p=0.15), other +5.42 pts (p=0.27). Logit interaction terms feature_035*race: all p>0.4. Hypothesis REFUTED: feature_035 effect is consistent across races; the apparent univariate race association is explained by clinical confounders, not differential drug effect.

proposed_hypotheses.text: The effect of feature_011 (a continuous prognostic marker) on objective_response varies by race.

analyses.result_summary: feature_011 effect within each race shows similar negative slopes; mean diff (responder vs non-responder) is -0.75 in asian, -0.64 in black, -0.57 in hispanic, -0.46 in other, -0.54 in white. Interaction p-values for feature_011 * race in logit model: all p>0.37. Hypothesis REFUTED: feature_011 effect is homogeneous across races.

proposed_hypotheses.text: The effect of feature_035 is modified by at least one of the continuous predictors (interaction effect on objective_response).

analyses.code: for cont in cont_top: sm.Logit(y, sm.add_constant(df[['feature_035', cont, f035*cont]].astype(float))).fit()
analyses.result_summary: Pairwise feature_035 x continuous interactions. Only feature_035 x feature_099 marginally significant (interaction coef -0.187, p=0.024). All other continuous interactions p>0.20. Effect of feature_035 is largely independent of continuous prognostic markers.

proposed_hypotheses.text: Among the top binary features, at least one pairwise binary x binary interaction is significant: the effect of one feature on objective_response depends on another binary feature.

analyses.code: for pairs in combinations(top_bins,2): sm.Logit(y, sm.add_constant(df[[a,b,a*b]])).fit()
analyses.result_summary: Pairwise binary interactions among top 6 binaries. Two strongly significant: (1) feature_035 x feature_014 interaction coef +0.730, p=7.7e-18; (2) feature_093 x feature_121 interaction coef +0.421, p=3.7e-3. The first is a major signal: when feature_014=1, feature_035 raises response by 13.4 pts; when feature_014=0, by only 0.6 pts.

proposed_hypotheses.text: Beyond feature_014, additional binary features modify the feature_035 effect (i.e., feature_035 acts as a treatment whose efficacy depends on multiple biomarkers).

analyses.code: for c in binary_cols: sm.Logit(y, sm.add_constant(df[['feature_035', c, f035*c]])).fit()
analyses.result_summary: Scan of feature_035 x all binary features. Strongest interactions: feature_035 x feature_027 coef +1.046, p=1.1e-32 (even stronger than feature_014); feature_035 x feature_014 coef +0.730, p=7.7e-18; feature_035 x feature_058 coef +0.700, p=1.0e-3; feature_035 x feature_124 coef +0.327, p=3.8e-3. All effect modifiers raise feature_035 efficacy when present. Hypothesis SUPPORTED: feature_035's effect is conditional on multiple biomarker features.

proposed_hypotheses.text: feature_014 and feature_027 together determine the efficacy of feature_035 (joint biomarker requirement). Specifically, the three-way interaction feature_014 x feature_027 x feature_035 is significant on objective_response.
Within the biomarker-pair subgroup (feature_014=1 AND feature_027=1), feature_035 produces a large absolute increase in objective_response that is robust to adjustment for clinical predictors.

analyses.result_summary: n=6025 in biomarker-pair subgroup. Unadjusted: feature_035=1 response 62.06% vs feature_035=0 16.08% (absolute diff +46.0 pts). Adjusted logit: feature_035 coef +2.180 (OR=8.85, 95% CI 7.17-10.93, p=2.2e-91). Other covariates retain their direction (feature_011 -0.037 p<1e-4; feature_006 -0.005 p=3e-3). Hypothesis STRONGLY SUPPORTED.

proposed_hypotheses.text: Within the biomarker-pair subgroup, feature_035 efficacy varies by patient race (a potential bias signal in treatment delivery or response).

analyses.code: sub2 = sub.copy(); X = pd.get_dummies(sub2[['feature_005']],drop_first=True); add f035*race; sm.Logit().fit()
analyses.result_summary: Within biomarker-pair (n=6025), feature_035 effect by race (absolute increase in response rate): white +48.4 pts (n=281 treated), hispanic +45.7 pts (n=73), asian +46.7 pts (n=19), black +33.6 pts (n=51), other +20.0 pts (n=3). Logit interaction terms feature_035*race: largest is feature_035*black with coef -0.424 (p=0.47); none individually significant. Numerically smaller benefit in black patients but small N (51 treated) and within sampling noise. h16 NOT SUPPORTED at conventional significance.

proposed_hypotheses.text: feature_035 is differentially administered to patients of different races within the biomarker-pair subgroup (a treatment-access bias).

analyses.result_summary: feature_035 prevalence within biomarker-pair, by race: white 7.25%, hispanic 7.68%, black 7.02%, asian 5.46%, other 2.44%. Chi-square p=0.19 (not significant). Overall (full cohort) feature_035 prevalence by race: 7.18%-7.51% across all groups, p=0.94. h17 NOT SUPPORTED: no statistically significant treatment-access disparity by race.

proposed_hypotheses.text: feature_035 is differentially administered by insurance type within the biomarker-pair subgroup.

analyses.result_summary: feature_035 prevalence within biomarker-pair, by insurance: medicare 7.43%, medicaid 7.13%, private 6.84%, uninsured 6.02%. Chi-square p=0.78. Numerically lowest in uninsured but small N (15 of 249 treated). h18 NOT SUPPORTED.

proposed_hypotheses.text: Within the biomarker-pair subgroup, feature_035 efficacy is modified by patient age (feature_078) -- the magnitude of benefit varies along the age distribution.

analyses.result_summary: Within biomarker-pair subgroup, feature_035 x feature_078 interaction coef +0.0259, p=9.8e-3. Direction is positive: the older the patient, the larger the absolute benefit of feature_035 (within this subgroup). Interesting given that feature_078 has no main effect on response in the full cohort. Suggests feature_035 may particularly help older biomarker-pair patients.

proposed_hypotheses.text: Within the biomarker-pair subgroup, lab markers (continuous features) modify feature_035 efficacy.

analyses.result_summary: Within biomarker-pair, scan of feature_035 x continuous interactions (top 5): feature_086 +0.139 p=7.1e-3 (effect grows with feature_086); feature_078 +0.026 p=9.8e-3; feature_094 -0.085 p=1.7e-2 (effect smaller with high feature_094, suggesting sodium-like marker matters); feature_037 +0.092 p=1.9e-2; feature_011 -0.064 p=2.3e-2 (drug works less well at high feature_011). Multiple effect modifiers; the strongest single signal is feature_011, consistent with feature_011 being a general prognostic marker.

proposed_hypotheses.text: feature_093 and feature_121 also interact: when both are present, response is higher than either alone.

analyses.result_summary: After adjustment for top continuous predictors, feature_093 x feature_121 interaction coef +0.433, p=3.0e-3. Cell rates: both 0 = 16.92%; only feature_093=1 = 18.11%; only feature_121=1 = 15.03%; both =1 = 22.62%. feature_121 alone is associated with lower response, but in combination with feature_093 it elevates response. h21 SUPPORTED.

proposed_hypotheses.text: The continuous predictors interact with each other -- the joint effect of feature_006 and feature_011 on objective_response is non-additive.

analyses.code: for pair: sm.Logit(y, sm.add_constant(df[[a,b,a*b]])).fit(); 2x2 median split
analyses.result_summary: Continuous-continuous interactions among top 5 predictors. feature_006 x feature_092 marginally significant (coef -1.4e-4, p=5.6e-3). feature_011 x feature_006 p=9.3e-2. Median-split 2x2 of feature_011 x feature_006 shows both-low cell at 20.1% and both-high at 14.1% (additive-ish). Modest evidence of non-additivity for feature_006 x feature_092.

proposed_hypotheses.text: There is a second targeted-therapy-like triad in the data analogous to feature_035 x feature_014 x feature_027.

analyses.code: for trio in combinations(candidate_binaries, 3) excluding feature_035: triple-interaction logit
analyses.result_summary: Scan of triple interactions excluding feature_035. Strongest: (feature_093, feature_058, feature_124) coef +1.291, p=2.6e-3. Others much weaker: (feature_093, feature_012, feature_058) p=7.2e-3; (feature_012, feature_027, feature_058) p=8.5e-3. None approach the magnitude of the feature_014 x feature_027 x feature_035 triad (p=1.4e-29). h23 only weakly supported; no second triad of comparable strength.

proposed_hypotheses.text: feature_011 (the strongest continuous predictor of non-response) shows a monotonic dose-response relationship across its full distribution.

analyses.result_summary: feature_011 quintile response rates: Q1 (-0.001 to 1.8) 18.61%; Q2 (1.8-4.3) 17.62%; Q3 (4.3-7.2) 16.31%; Q4 (7.2-23.6) 13.27%. Note the distribution is irregular: Q1 has ~20k patients (many zeros). Monotonic decrease across quintiles. At feature_011>10, response rate drops below 12%. h24 SUPPORTED.

proposed_hypotheses.text: feature_006 (suspected performance/disease-burden marker) shows a monotonic dose-response with objective_response.

analyses.result_summary: feature_006 quintile response rates: Q1 (20-43.3) 18.74%; Q2 (43.3-55) 18.45%; Q3 (55-65) 16.55%; Q4 (65-76.8) 15.88%; Q5 (76.8-100) 14.87%. Monotonic decrease (~4 pt absolute drop across distribution). Robust under adjustment (multivariable OR per unit 0.994, p<1e-19). h25 SUPPORTED.

proposed_hypotheses.text: After excluding the biomarker-pair subgroup that responds strongly to feature_035, the remaining clinical predictors of objective_response are feature_011, feature_006, feature_063, feature_092, feature_099 (i.e., they are robust prognostic markers, not just confounders of feature_035).

analyses.result_summary: In the population excluding the triple-positive responders (n=49573), the same multivariable model still shows: feature_011 -0.041 (p<1e-30); feature_006 -0.006 (p<1e-19); feature_092 -0.002 (p<1e-4); feature_063 -0.007 (p<1e-5); feature_099 +0.117 (p<1e-5). Effect sizes essentially unchanged. h26 SUPPORTED: these are prognostic markers that operate independently of the targeted-therapy effect.

proposed_hypotheses.text: Synthesis: The dominant clinically-meaningful pattern in this AML cohort is a targeted-therapy effect: patients with the joint biomarker pattern feature_014=1 AND feature_027=1 who receive feature_035 have a ~46 percentage-point absolute increase in objective_response (62% vs 16%), while feature_035 has essentially no effect outside this biomarker-defined subgroup. Independent prognostic markers of response are feature_011 (worse with higher), feature_006 (worse with higher), feature_063 (worse with higher), feature_092 (worse with higher), feature_099 (better with higher). Patient age (feature_078), race, and insurance have negligible effects on response after adjustment, with no evidence of differential treatment access or efficacy.

analyses.code: Final saturated logit including triple interaction and main confounders
analyses.result_summary: Final model fit. Triple-interaction term (f014*f027*f035) coef ~+2.1 (p<1e-25 with adjustment); within biomarker-pair subset, feature_035 OR=8.85 (95% CI 7.17-10.93). Continuous prognostic predictors retain their effects across all model specifications and across all major patient subgroups. Bias signals (race, insurance differential receipt or differential efficacy) are not statistically supported. h27 SUPPORTED as the main conclusion.
